---
title: "Background"
---

This site monitors **open-access publishing** at the
[TU9](https://www.tu9.de/) universities of technology, using
[OpenAlex](https://openalex.org/) and snapshotting the figures over time. The
headline metric is the open-access share of each university's
**corresponding-author** output; a few entity-level indicators are kept as
context. Nothing is scraped work by work — the counts come from OpenAlex's
server-side aggregation.

## Which institutions

The tracked institutions are the nine universities of the TU9 alliance — one
OpenAlex institution each. Affiliated organisations such as university hospitals
and research institutes are **not** tracked as separate institutions; where they
belong to a university they enter only through the
[consolidated view](#consolidated-view-leiden-ranking) below. The full list,
with OpenAlex and [ROR](https://ror.org/) IDs, is in
[`data-raw/institutions.csv`](https://github.com/slub/tu9-openalex/blob/main/data-raw/institutions.csv)
and can be edited to change the selection.

## Open access on the corresponding-author lens

The denominator is not a university's whole output but the works whose
*corresponding author* is affiliated with it — OpenAlex's
[`corresponding_institution_ids`](https://blog.openalex.org/a-big-improvement-to-our-corresponding-author-data/)
filter, recently much improved. This is the lens that matches
[OpenAPC](https://openapc.net/) and transformative-agreement accounting, where
the corresponding author's institution carries the article charge, and it is the
figure relevant to a possible OA monitoring.

Every OA figure is prefixed `ca_` to mark it as corresponding-author-level:

- **`ca_works`** — works with a corresponding author at the university, by
  publication year;
- **`ca_oa_works`** / **`ca_oa_share`** — of those, how many (and what fraction)
  are open access (`is_oa:true`);
- a **headline** over a multi-year period (works published in the last complete
  years) — a more robust number than any single year — plus the single-year
  reference figure;
- the **OA-status composition** for the reference year — the gold / hybrid /
  green / bronze / diamond / closed split, which shows how much of the OA share
  runs through the hybrid and gold routes that transformative agreements target.

- **`ca_doaj_works`** / **`ca_doaj_share`** — of the corresponding-author works,
  how many appeared in a journal listed in the
  [DOAJ](https://doaj.org/), via `primary_location.source.is_in_doaj:true`.

DOAJ is reported **separately from the OA-status composition**, not as one of its
categories — though not because it sits at a different level. `gold` and
`diamond` are themselves journal properties: both mean the work appeared in a
fully open-access journal, which is close to what the DOAJ flag records
(`diamond` adds that the journal levies no APC). Only `hybrid`, `bronze`, `green`
and `closed` turn on the individual record — its licence, and whether a free copy
exists anywhere.

The reason to keep DOAJ out is structural rather than conceptual: the OA statuses
are a **partition** — every work has exactly one, and they sum to the works total
— whereas DOAJ listing is an independent yes/no that cuts across them. A
DOAJ-listed work is normally gold or diamond, yet the two do not coincide even
where they overlap most: for TU Dresden in 2025, 543 corresponding-author works
appeared in DOAJ-listed journals while 788 were classified gold or diamond. The
gap runs in the direction one would expect if OpenAlex treats more journals as
fully open access than DOAJ currently lists, but we have not traced it
title by title. Folding DOAJ in as a seventh row would count those works twice —
once under the status they already hold — and break the sum that validation
enforces.

All of this comes from OpenAlex's server-side `group_by` aggregation (per-year
and per-status counts), so no individual works are downloaded.

Whether this corresponding-author OA share is a reliable enough basis for
routine OA monitoring is exactly the question this dataset is meant to help
answer.

## Consolidated view (Leiden Ranking)

Whether a university hospital or an affiliated institute counts as part of "the
university" is a judgement call. The
[CWTS Leiden Ranking Open Edition](https://open.leidenranking.com/) makes that
judgement explicitly: it links affiliated organisations to a core university as
`component` (weight 1, counted fully — e.g. the university hospital), `joint`
(fractional), or `associated` (listed but not counted). The TU9 slice of that
mapping is extracted, reproducibly, by `scripts/leiden_affiliations.py` into
[`data-raw/leiden_affiliations.csv`](https://github.com/slub/tu9-openalex/blob/main/data-raw/leiden_affiliations.csv)
(source: CWTS Leiden Ranking Open Edition 2025 — Data,
[Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/records/17471989), CC0; OpenAlex snapshot 08/2025).

So each university appears **two ways**: as the single OpenAlex institution
(identified by its ROR id), and *consolidated* — its OpenAlex id OR-ed with those
of its weight-1 `component` affiliates in one `corresponding_institution_ids`
filter, so a work still counts once. The consolidated unit is where, for
instance, the
[University Hospital Carl Gustav Carus](https://openalex.org/I4210162051) is
counted inside TU Dresden and the
[Universitätsklinikum Aachen](https://openalex.org/I4210120689) inside RWTH.
Fractional `joint` affiliates are not (yet) included — only full components. A
university without any consolidates to itself, so all nine have a consolidated
value and the two views stay comparable across the alliance.

## Core-source-filtered view (CWTS Core sources)

A third view keeps the same Leiden-consolidated member set — the university's
OpenAlex institution id OR-ed with its weight-1 `component` affiliates — but
additionally restricts works to sources on the CWTS Core sources allow-list using
OpenAlex's `primary_location.source.is_core:true` filter. This gives
**corresponding-author works in CWTS Core sources** and their open-access share.

The OpenAlex filter is applied literally as:

```
primary_location.source.is_core:true
```

combined with the corresponding-author lens
(`corresponding_institution_ids:<university-and-component-ids>`) and the usual
XPAC exclusion (`is_xpac:false`). The numerator additionally requires
`is_oa:true`. The `primary_location` form is used deliberately so a work
qualifies only when its primary venue is a Core source, not because a secondary
or repository location is associated with one.

This view is meaningful even for universities without Leiden components
because the source filter itself narrows the publication universe. It is
populated for all nine TU9 universities.

Important caveat: this is a **source-only Core filter**, not a reproduction of
the official Leiden Ranking `core publication` set. The official CWTS definition
of a core publication applies additional publication-level criteria, including
language, work type, source type, references, affiliation availability and
retraction status. The Zenodo records below provide the versioned source list
and methodology; the live OpenAlex `is_core` flag may evolve after the August
2025 snapshot.

### Provenance

- "Core sources and core publications in OpenAlex", CWTS, 2025.
  DOI: <https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17200868>. Standalone source list
  (`source.tsv`) including `source_id`, `source`, `source_type`, `issn_l`,
  `is_core_source`, `n_works` and `n_core_works`, based on the August 2025
  OpenAlex snapshot.
- "A methodology for identifying core sources and core publications in OpenAlex",
  CWTS, 2025. DOI: <https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17200830>. Methodology report
  plus the supplementary source spreadsheet (`Supplementary material -
  Sources.ods`).
- CWTS Leiden Ranking Open Edition 2025 — Data.
  DOI: <https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17471989>. The `pub.tsv` table includes an
  `is_core_pub` publication-level field, but the standalone, auditable source
  list is the separate Core-sources dataset above.

## Context metrics from the institution entity

Beside the OA figures, each snapshot keeps a few entity-level indicators for
context. They appear only on an individual university's own page; neither the
per-university table on the home page nor the alliance summary under
Institutions uses them:

- **works_count** and **works per year** — the university's total output;
- **cited_by_count**, **h-index**, **i10-index**, **2-year mean citedness**.

The complete OpenAlex entity is also archived as raw JSON per snapshot
(`data/snapshots/<slug>/<date>.json`) for anything not surfaced in the CSVs.

### Why these figures differ from the OpenAlex profile

An institution's page on openalex.org links to its works with
`authorships.institutions.lineage:<id>&include_xpac=true`. That is a wider
population than the one counted here, in two independent ways, so the numbers
will not match unless both are accounted for. For TU Dresden:

| Lens | Works |
|------|------:|
| `authorships.institutions.id`, XPAC excluded — **published here** | 129,281 |
| `authorships.institutions.id`, XPAC included — the entity's `works_count` | 139,032 |
| `authorships.institutions.lineage`, XPAC included — **what openalex.org links to** | 148,325 |

The first step is the Expansion Pack. The second is **lineage**: OpenAlex's
parent/child hierarchy, which rolls a university's child institutions into it.
This dataset counts the institution itself — `corresponding_institution_ids` and
`authorships.institutions.id` match one entity and perform no lineage expansion
(verified: adding a child institution to the filter increases the count).

The lineage figure is carried in `metrics.csv` as
`works_count_lineage_incl_xpac` purely so the two can be reconciled; no metric on
this site is derived from it.

Lineage is also **not** interchangeable with the Leiden consolidation. They
overlap but diverge exactly where it matters: the
[University Hospital Carl Gustav Carus](https://openalex.org/I4210162051) is
*not* in TU Dresden's OpenAlex lineage (OpenAlex links it as `related`, not
`child`), yet Leiden counts it as a `component`. Conversely an institute such as
[NaMLab](https://openalex.org/I4210122489) *is* in the lineage. Using lineage
would therefore quietly drop the university hospital — which is why the
consolidated view is built from the Leiden mapping instead.

### Expansion Pack (XPAC) is excluded

In November 2025 OpenAlex added ~190 million works as the
[Expansion Pack](https://developers.openalex.org/guides/key-concepts#xpac-expansion-pack)
(XPAC) — mainly datasets and repository records with lower average data quality.
Every works query here appends `is_xpac:false`, so `works_count`, the
works-per-year counts and all open-access figures exclude XPAC explicitly rather
than by relying on OpenAlex's current default. Because the institution *entity*'s
own aggregates include XPAC, `works_count` and works-per-year are taken from the
works API (not the entity). The citation-based indicators (`cited_by_count`,
h-index, i10-index, 2-year mean citedness) are the exception: OpenAlex publishes
them only as an entity aggregate, so those figures **include** XPAC.

### Entity aggregates lag the live API

The institution entity's own figures — `works_count`, `counts_by_year`,
`cited_by_count` and the summary statistics — are precomputed aggregates, while
every works query here is answered live. The two are therefore not from the same
moment. For a year that is still filling up this is visible: in July 2026 the live
XPAC-*excluded* count of TU Munich's 2026 works (9,497) already exceeded the
entity's precomputed XPAC-*inclusive* figure for the same year (9,431).

That is a property of the data, not an error, but it means the two sources should
not be compared as though they were simultaneous. The consistency checks in
`scripts/validate.R` compare live figures with live figures for exactly this
reason.

## How it is assembled

1. For every university in `data-raw/institutions.csv`, the pipeline runs a
   handful of OpenAlex
   [works](https://developers.openalex.org/api-reference/works) `group_by`
   queries — corresponding-author works by year and OA status, total works
   by year, the Leiden-consolidated corresponding-author view, and the
   Leiden-consolidated view restricted to CWTS Core sources
   (`primary_location.source.is_core:true`) — and reads the
   [institution entity](https://developers.openalex.org/api-reference/institutions)
   for the citation indicators, using the polite pool and, where configured, a
   free OpenAlex API key.
2. Everything is fetched into memory first and only then validated: the exact
   configured institution set, every required product per university, a single
   consistent snapshot date, and the arithmetic behind each OA share. A coverage
   guard rail additionally aborts if the total works count drops sharply against
   the previous snapshot. If any check fails, nothing is written and the
   published data is left untouched — a degraded run never publishes.
3. Only after that are the files written: a dated row per university is appended
   to `data/metrics.csv` (re-running on the same day replaces that day's rows
   rather than duplicating them), the publication-year and OA views are rewritten
   for the latest snapshot, and `meta.json` is regenerated. Products that no
   longer apply are removed rather than left behind as stale files.

## Caveats

OpenAlex coverage and attribution evolve continuously — affiliation strings are
disambiguated over time, and works are added or reassigned. A change between two
snapshots therefore reflects both real publishing activity and improvements in
OpenAlex's own data. Figures are best read as a consistent, reproducible series
rather than as an authoritative count.
